id: Buitenreclame komt tot bloei in digitale tijdperk__a9-hybrid-small-to-big__0004
text: ABN AMRO expects total out-of-home revenues to grow by 5 per cent in 2025 and 6 per cent in 2026. Up to 2030, out-of-home revenues are expected to grow by an average of 5 per cent a year and overtake radio as an advertising channel.

Up to 2030, out-of-home revenues are expected to grow by an average of 5 per cent a year and overtake radio as an advertising channel. The Netherlands has 83,000 out-of-home objects, such as bus shelters, motorway masts, transformer houses, scaffold wraps, blank walls, billboards, information pillars and lamp posts. Even buses, trams and ferries are used as advertising platforms.

id: Buitenreclame komt tot bloei in digitale tijdperk__a9-hybrid-small-to-big__0014
text: Digital out-of-home is booming and offers new possibilities. In three years, digital out-of-home revenue has more than doubled to 172 million euros in 2024, according to VIA/Deloitte’s Digital Ad Spend 2024 report.

metadata.chunk_id: Buitenreclame komt tot bloei in digitale tijdperk__a9-hybrid-small-to-big__0014
metadata.source_slug: Buitenreclame komt tot bloei in digitale tijdperk
metadata.source_title: Out-of-home advertising flourishes in the digital era
metadata.tags: Analyse, Technologie, Media & Telecom, Retail, Digitalisering
metadata.author: Mario Bersem
metadata.description: Discover how out-of-home advertising is growing in the digital era, with trends, figures, innovations and the role of municipalities and operators. Read more.
metadata.parent_text: Digital out-of-home is booming and offers new possibilities. In three years, digital out-of-home revenue has more than doubled to 172 million euros in 2024, according to VIA/Deloitte’s Digital Ad Spend 2024 report. In 2025, revenue will grow by 4 per cent to around 179 million euros, ABN AMRO expects. That is 56 per cent of total out-of-home revenue in 2025, which is expected to be 317 million euros. This is striking, as only 17 per cent of all advertising objects are digital; the remaining 83 per cent are analogue. Digital screens generate more revenue than analogue ones because they are on average larger, in the busiest locations and attract more attention. Digital screens attract more attention thanks to moving images and rotating ads.

metadata.tags: Analyse, Technologie, Media & Telecom, Retail, Digitalisering
metadata.author: Mario Bersem
metadata.description: Discover how out-of-home advertising is growing in the digital era, with trends, figures, innovations and the role of municipalities and operators. Read more.
metadata.parent_text: Digital out-of-home is booming and offers new possibilities. In three years, digital out-of-home revenue has more than doubled to 172 million euros in 2024, according to VIA/Deloitte’s Digital Ad Spend 2024 report. In 2025, revenue will grow by 4 per cent to around 179 million euros, ABN AMRO expects. That is 56 per cent of total out-of-home revenue in 2025, which is expected to be 317 million euros. This is striking, as only 17 per cent of all advertising objects are digital; the remaining 83 per cent are analogue. Digital screens generate more revenue than analogue ones because they are on average larger, in the busiest locations and attract more attention. Digital screens attract more attention thanks to moving images and rotating ads.

id: Buitenreclame komt tot bloei in digitale tijdperk__a9-hybrid-small-to-big__0025
text: Most new digital out-of-home screens are indoors, in shop windows. As long as the screens are visible from the street or public space, they count towards the out-of-home inventory.

metadata.chunk_id: Buitenreclame komt tot bloei in digitale tijdperk__a9-hybrid-small-to-big__0025
metadata.source_slug: Buitenreclame komt tot bloei in digitale tijdperk
metadata.source_title: Out-of-home advertising flourishes in the digital era
metadata.tags: Analyse, Technologie, Media & Telecom, Retail, Digitalisering
metadata.author: Mario Bersem
metadata.description: Discover how out-of-home advertising is growing in the digital era, with trends, figures, innovations and the role of municipalities and operators. Read more.
metadata.parent_text: Most new digital out-of-home screens are indoors, in shop windows. As long as the screens are visible from the street or public space, they count towards the out-of-home inventory. This phenomenon is part of the [rise of retail media](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/sectoren-en-trends/technologie/advertenties-lucratieve-bijverdienste-voor-winkelketens.html), where grocers and other retailers are turning to advertising as a lucrative side business. For example, HEMA has almost 330 digital screens with a range of campaigns that generate money in the evenings when the shops are closed. Global, a large out-of-home operator, manages ad sales for HEMA.

id: Buitenreclame komt tot bloei in digitale tijdperk__a9-hybrid-small-to-big__0033
text: Around 30 per cent of digital ad space is placed automatically using algorithms that take into account factors such as time of day, location and weather. This share is rising and points to more efficient and flexible use of digital ad space.

metadata.chunk_id: Buitenreclame komt tot bloei in digitale tijdperk__a9-hybrid-small-to-big__0033
metadata.source_slug: Buitenreclame komt tot bloei in digitale tijdperk
metadata.source_title: Out-of-home advertising flourishes in the digital era
metadata.tags: Analyse, Technologie, Media & Telecom, Retail, Digitalisering
metadata.author: Mario Bersem
metadata.description: Discover how out-of-home advertising is growing in the digital era, with trends, figures, innovations and the role of municipalities and operators. Read more.
metadata.parent_text: Around 30 per cent of digital ad space is placed automatically using algorithms that take into account factors such as time of day, location and weather. This share is rising and points to more efficient and flexible use of digital ad space. Digital advertising dominates the media landscape. Digital ads, especially those via web pages, social media, streaming services and the digital screens mentioned, dominate the Dutch media landscape and accounted for 4.1 billion euros in 2024 — almost three-quarters of all ad spend. Big tech companies, with a market share of 80 per cent (3.5 billion euros), dominate the digital ad market. The digital transformation of the media is not yet complete, so the share of digital ads is still rising. While traditional mass media such as television, radio and newspapers are losing reach, out-of-home — also known as ‘out of home’ — is gaining importance. Johan Smit, Director of PMA, the industry association for media agencies, explains: “Out of home is actually the only mass medium left and is thereby taking over the leading role of linear TV.”. Digital out-of-home stands out from other digital media, such as online ads, because it is usually not perceived as intrusive. Online, ad blockers can be used and some people experience ad fatigue. “Out-of-home is, on the one hand, not intrusive and, on the other, impossible to miss,” says Sita Bakker, Director of OUTREACH, the industry association for out-of-home operators. “It does not interrupt people during their activities, which contributes to a positive experience,” Bakker adds. Analogue out-of-home also remains strong in the digital era. Analogue out-of-home remains relevant and is even growing, with a projected 6 per cent rise in revenue in 2025. That puts analogue out-of-home revenue at around 138 million euros, ABN AMRO expects.

metadata.source_title: Out-of-home advertising flourishes in the digital era
metadata.tags: Analyse, Technologie, Media & Telecom, Retail, Digitalisering
metadata.author: Mario Bersem
metadata.description: Discover how out-of-home advertising is growing in the digital era, with trends, figures, innovations and the role of municipalities and operators. Read more.
metadata.parent_text: Around 30 per cent of digital ad space is placed automatically using algorithms that take into account factors such as time of day, location and weather. This share is rising and points to more efficient and flexible use of digital ad space. Digital advertising dominates the media landscape. Digital ads, especially those via web pages, social media, streaming services and the digital screens mentioned, dominate the Dutch media landscape and accounted for 4.1 billion euros in 2024 — almost three-quarters of all ad spend. Big tech companies, with a market share of 80 per cent (3.5 billion euros), dominate the digital ad market. The digital transformation of the media is not yet complete, so the share of digital ads is still rising. While traditional mass media such as television, radio and newspapers are losing reach, out-of-home — also known as ‘out of home’ — is gaining importance. Johan Smit, Director of PMA, the industry association for media agencies, explains: “Out of home is actually the only mass medium left and is thereby taking over the leading role of linear TV.”. Digital out-of-home stands out from other digital media, such as online ads, because it is usually not perceived as intrusive. Online, ad blockers can be used and some people experience ad fatigue. “Out-of-home is, on the one hand, not intrusive and, on the other, impossible to miss,” says Sita Bakker, Director of OUTREACH, the industry association for out-of-home operators. “It does not interrupt people during their activities, which contributes to a positive experience,” Bakker adds. Analogue out-of-home also remains strong in the digital era. Analogue out-of-home remains relevant and is even growing, with a projected 6 per cent rise in revenue in 2025. That puts analogue out-of-home revenue at around 138 million euros, ABN AMRO expects.

text: These examples show further growth potential for out-of-home, which is also evident from experience in other countries. In a positive scenario, in which current trends continue strongly, the out-of-home share could rise to 7.5 per cent and revenues could reach 600 million euros in 2030.

metadata.source_title: Out-of-home advertising flourishes in the digital era
metadata.tags: Analyse, Technologie, Media & Telecom, Retail, Digitalisering
metadata.author: Mario Bersem
metadata.description: Discover how out-of-home advertising is growing in the digital era, with trends, figures, innovations and the role of municipalities and operators. Read more.
metadata.parent_text: These examples show further growth potential for out-of-home, which is also evident from experience in other countries. In a positive scenario, in which current trends continue strongly, the out-of-home share could rise to 7.5 per cent and revenues could reach 600 million euros in 2030. In the base case, however, the out-of-home share remains stable at around the current 5 per cent. Municipalities set out-of-home policy. Out-of-home targets passers-by, on foot or by bike, in cars or on public transport. Municipalities apply strict rules on where and how advertising is allowed, that is, in which size and, for example, the brightness of digital screens. Some municipalities levy a municipal advertising tax. Because municipalities are responsible for the design of public space, rules vary widely: the Netherlands has 342 municipalities, each with its own policy on out-of-home. For instance, the Municipality of The Hague has banned ‘fossil advertising’. Through a general municipal by-law (APV), The Hague bans ads for air travel, petrol cars and fossil energy contracts.

id: Buitenreclame komt tot bloei in digitale tijdperk__a9-hybrid-small-to-big__0066
text: Municipalities award multi-year concessions for out-of-home through public tenders. In return, they receive a financial contribution, often combined with payments in kind such as street furniture or media space for their own campaigns and charities.

metadata.chunk_id: Buitenreclame komt tot bloei in digitale tijdperk__a9-hybrid-small-to-big__0066
metadata.source_slug: Buitenreclame komt tot bloei in digitale tijdperk
metadata.source_title: Out-of-home advertising flourishes in the digital era
metadata.tags: Analyse, Technologie, Media & Telecom, Retail, Digitalisering
metadata.author: Mario Bersem
metadata.description: Discover how out-of-home advertising is growing in the digital era, with trends, figures, innovations and the role of municipalities and operators. Read more.
metadata.parent_text: Municipalities award multi-year concessions for out-of-home through public tenders. In return, they receive a financial contribution, often combined with payments in kind such as street furniture or media space for their own campaigns and charities. JCDecaux is an example of an operator that invests in bus shelters and maintains them in return for advertising exploitation. Notably, JCDecaux has more staff working in the technical department than in ad sales. A [PWC study](https://www.outsmart.org.uk/resources/ooh-supporting-uk-society) in the United Kingdom shows that 46 per cent of out-of-home ad revenue flows back to municipalities. Applied to the Dutch market, this amounts to around 146 million euros in 2025. Out-of-home largely depends on municipal policy and concessions, which vary widely between the 342 Dutch municipalities. While some municipalities impose specific restrictions, such as the fossil ad ban in The Hague, there is no national guideline.

metadata.author: Mario Bersem
metadata.description: Discover how out-of-home advertising is growing in the digital era, with trends, figures, innovations and the role of municipalities and operators. Read more.
metadata.parent_text: Municipalities award multi-year concessions for out-of-home through public tenders. In return, they receive a financial contribution, often combined with payments in kind such as street furniture or media space for their own campaigns and charities. JCDecaux is an example of an operator that invests in bus shelters and maintains them in return for advertising exploitation. Notably, JCDecaux has more staff working in the technical department than in ad sales. A [PWC study](https://www.outsmart.org.uk/resources/ooh-supporting-uk-society) in the United Kingdom shows that 46 per cent of out-of-home ad revenue flows back to municipalities. Applied to the Dutch market, this amounts to around 146 million euros in 2025. Out-of-home largely depends on municipal policy and concessions, which vary widely between the 342 Dutch municipalities. While some municipalities impose specific restrictions, such as the fossil ad ban in The Hague, there is no national guideline.

text: In 1964, Jean Claude Decaux introduced his first bus shelters in Lyon. In return for funding, installation and maintenance, his company, JCDecaux, obtained the rights to exploit advertising in these ‘abribus’ (French for bus shelter).

metadata.tags: Analyse, Technologie, Media & Telecom, Retail, Digitalisering
metadata.author: Mario Bersem
metadata.description: Discover how out-of-home advertising is growing in the digital era, with trends, figures, innovations and the role of municipalities and operators. Read more.
metadata.parent_text: In 1964, Jean Claude Decaux introduced his first bus shelters in Lyon. In return for funding, installation and maintenance, his company, JCDecaux, obtained the rights to exploit advertising in these ‘abribus’ (French for bus shelter). This public-private partnership model spread worldwide, and the term ‘abri’ became synonymous with a widely used ad format: 1,185 mm x 1,750 mm, around 2 m². Advertising in abri format accounts for 65 per cent of all out-of-home in the Netherlands and can be found in bus shelters across the country, stations, shopping centres and along busy streets.
